dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain.
Good morning, ma'am. I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I have chest pain and shortness of breath. It started about six hours ago, in the early morning.
Can you open your eyes for me? Are you able to tell me your name and where you are right now?
My name is Mary. I live in Richmond.
Good. Can you tell me what time it is?
I think it's about, uh, ten o'clock.
Can you squeeze my fingers with both of your hands for me? Great, that helps me know how you're doing.
I note your airway is patent and unobstructed, and we can proceed safely.
Her respirations are shallow and slightly rapid. I'll take a count while I check her oxygen saturation.
Respirations are at 44 per minute.
Oxygen saturation is 72 percent.
I've attached the ECG monitor - it's showing a sinus rhythm with a right bundle branch block.
I'll take a look at her radial pulse next. Skin is pale, cool, and dry.
Pulse is 94 beats per minute. Strong and regular.
Blood pressure is 126 over 74.
Mary, can you tell me what time the pain first started and what you might have been doing at the time?
It woke me up, so ... around 0530, I guess.
Can you describe the pain to me? What does it feel like?
Sharp, burning, localization right now, centered in my chest, and it worsens when I inhale deeply.
Do you have any other symptoms, like dizziness, or nausea?
I had a little bit of nausea earlier this morning and even had one round of diarrhea.
Have you taken any medications recently?
Yes, I gave her two doses of nitroglycerin an hour and a half apart.
How old are you, Mary?
I'm 81 years old.
Got you. So, when you say the pain woke you up, it's been present for about six hours now?
Does anything make the pain better or worse, in terms of movement or position?
It gets worse when I inhale deeply.
Got it. And the pain catching the breath-it's localized to your chest or does it radiate?
No radiation, it's localized around my sternum.
On a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst possible pain, where would you rate the chest pain now?
Your Glasgow Coma Scale score is 15 - alert, oriented, and following commands.
You're alert, respond to verbal commands, and obey physical stimulation. So, A-4/V-4/M-4; you're fully responsive.
I see you have a history of COPD, old heart attack, and hypertension. How else are you managing your health?
Yes. She's had heart issues before-a heart attack in May and fluid around the lungs.
Your face is symmetrical.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive at 3mm.
Your skin color appears pale, and you're cool to the touch.
Any trouble with your nose or mouth today?
No, nothing unusual.
I've also noted no jugular vein distention and a normocephalic head shape.
Would you mind if I check the strength of your arms and legs?
No, go ahead.
Equal strength and sensation bilaterally on arms and legs.
I do note no pedal edema and that your strength is equally present on both sides. PMS is positive bilaterally.
For my lung assessment, can you take a deep breath in for me?
I'm hearing some wheezing in the bilateral apexes, and absent sounds at the bases.
Your chest rise and fall are minimal. Any pain with this test?
No more than the usual. The chest wall is stable and non-tender.
I've noted no abdominal distention. You don't seem to be experiencing acute stomach pain.
Is there anything else related to your chest, lungs, or heart that we should know about?
No, I'm focused on the chest pain.
I see your history includes end-stage renal disease and diabetes. Are you aware of any changes in your condition recently?
No changes actually, but she has vertigo sometimes and anxiety that haven't changed.
I am about to start a dual nebulizer treatment with Albuterol and Ipratropium for your difficulty breathing.
EKG is still showing right bundle branch block with a sinus rhythm.
Attempting to take a blood glucose level, but no result came up; it remains at zero.
Placing you on 8 LPM oxygen via nebulizer and applying 4 lead ECG monitoring per standing order.
Administering 5.0 mg of Albuterol and 0.5 mg of Ipratropium via nebulizer.
I will now attempt IV access in your arm.
I am performing IV start in the extremity vein - the right arm.
We are engaging in chest pain protocol 2-1 due to her presentation indicating a possible cardiac or respiratory issue.
Pulse dropped to 86 and is still regular.
Respirations decreased slightly to 34.
Blood pressure is now 149 over 89.
Pulse ox is improving to 86 percent.
I took a second glance at the glucose and detected 370 mg/dL. That's high, but no current signs of distress.
The breathing treatment seems to have helped marginally. Oxygen saturation is better.
We are still noting a zero on the handheld glucose meter, but we've documented the 370 mg/dL from a prior reading.
Glucose remains at zero on reassessment.
We administer 324mg of aspirin by mouth to help protect the suspected cardiac condition, but noted her diabetic status and high glucose.
We're heading to Memorial Regional Medical Center, and I'll let them know what we've done so far.
We've secured your movement onto the stretcher in a Fowler's position with shoulder and lap belts. Hospital was contacted earlier to prepare.
I'm okay with that. Thank you.
Pulse is now 93, slightly increased from previous.
Respirations are now 28.
Blood pressure is 161 over 94 now.
Pulse ox is 85 now.
Administered 8 LPM oxygen via mask to assist with breathing, and await improvement.
Pulse remains 94, and still regular.
Respirations now at 41.
Blood pressure now is 165 over 88.
Pulse ox reading is 84 percent on oxygen via mask.
Let me check your current medications to ensure none interact. Do you have any medications with you?
Yes, she used Humalog and Brilinta. She's allergic to Lisinopril.
We will document she is on levothyroxine, gabapentin, and not on Vasotec. She is allergic to it.
Other meds include: LASIX, Protonix, Voltaren, Synthroid, Phenergan, Senna Lax, Sucralfate, and VESICARE.
She is also on potassium and Atorvastatin. This will be important for hospital management.
She is also taking Diclofenac after meals under doctor instructions. She is currently on Promethazine for nausea as well.
12-lead ECG has now been obtained, showing anterior infarct signs with possible lateral ischemia.
The nebulizer treatments have had a minor benefit, but she has also received aspirin for cardiac protection.
Taking vitals again-pulse and BP remain stable, and SpO2 is slightly down but still acceptable.
Blood glucose was confirmed earlier to be 370 - we'll inform the hospital about diabetic management and insulin needed.
We've transferred you to the stretcher, and all equipment is ready for transport.
She is on Brilinta for her heart condition, and we will make sure that is documented for the hospital team.
Yes, she takes it for her heart condition.
We've sent a pre-arrival report to the hospital using the "Power On" system to alert them of her condition.
All buckles are secure for transport. She is stable, but we are closely monitoring.
Pulse ox is currently at 84 with oxygen via mask.
Respiratory rate is once again back to 41 per minute since we switched to mask delivery.
She's allergic to Crestor in addition to Lisinopril - is that right, Bystander?
Yes, she won't tolerate medications that include Crestor or Vasotec.
I see infectious sepsis listed as one of the field impressions. Have you noticed any infection symptoms, like fever or chills?
No, I haven't had any symptoms of infection. It's been chest pain and difficulty breathing.
The third pulse ox is 84 again after administering aspirin (ASA PO). We continue to monitor and reassess medications as needed.
